explanation_md: `refs[1]` wraps `b`. Calling `.get() = 20` writes 20 into `b` through the reference wrapper. `a` and `c` are unchanged, so the output is `1 20 3`.

prompt_md: Why can't you write `std::array<int&, 3> refs { a, b, c };`?

options.a: References are not objects — they have no size and cannot be stored in containers
options.b: The `<array>` header does not support reference element types
options.c: `std::array` only supports scalar types like int, double, or char
options.d: You need `std::ref()` on each element even for a raw reference array

explanation_md: C++ references are not objects — they are aliases that have no independent existence, no address, and no size. Container types like `std::array` require their element type to be an object type. `std::reference_wrapper<T>` solves this by wrapping the reference in a regular object.

The range-based for loop uses `int& val`, which receives an implicit conversion from each `std::reference_wrapper<int>`. Adding 3 to `val` modifies the underlying variables `x` and `y` directly. After the loop, `x` is 5+3=8 and `y` is 10+3=13.

prompt_md: What is the correct way to print the integer value stored via a `std::reference_wrapper<int>` called `rw`?

options.a: `std::cout << rw;` — the wrapper prints its value automatically
options.b: `std::cout << rw.get();` — use `.get()` to retrieve the underlying int
options.d: `std::cout << rw.value();` — use `.value()` to retrieve the int

explanation_md: `.get()` is the standard way to retrieve the underlying reference from a `std::reference_wrapper`. Streaming `rw` directly does not automatically convert to `int` for `std::cout`. There is no dereference operator or `.value()` method on `std::reference_wrapper`.

options.a: `refs` should be declared as `std::array<int&, 3>` for the loop to work
options.b: The range loop uses `int v` — this copies the value; `v *= 2` modifies the copy, not the original variables
options.c: `std::reference_wrapper` cannot be used with range-based for loops
options.d: The initializer `{ a, b, c }` should use `std::ref(a)` etc. or the array is invalid

explanation_md: When the range loop declares `int v`, the `std::reference_wrapper<int>` implicitly converts to `int`, producing a copy. Multiplying the copy has no effect on `a`, `b`, or `c`. Fix by writing `int& v` so the loop element binds as a reference to the underlying variable, and `v *= 2` modifies the original.
